claim: Dimitrij Ovtcharov's '4 Steps to learn the World's Best Tomahawk Serve' (Jan 2022) is a top-tier tutorial from a world-class pro who relies on this serve in match play.
quote: 4 Steps with Dimitrij Ovtcharov to learn the Worlds Best Tomahawk Serve (published January 11, 2022)

claim: PingSkills' 'Low Tomahawk Serve' video with coach Alois Rosario teaches keeping the ball low by getting the body down and contacting from below, which is essential for a short tomahawk.
quote: Low Tomahawk Serve | PingSkills | Table Tennis - YouTube ... A PingSkills video posted in February 2012

claim: PingSkills' Alois Rosario emphasises that the tomahawk action 'comes naturally to a lot of people' and that small bat-angle changes disguise spin variations on the same motion.
quote: the action comes naturally to a lot of people ... small changes of your bat angle ... you get low and contact the ball from lower

claim: Adam Bobrow's UTT 'Tomahawk Serve Tutorial' (Aug 2019) is a widely cited entertainment-flavoured tutorial that walks through the throwing-axe motion for amateurs.
quote: Demo Zone segment at UTT (Ultimate Table Tennis, the Indian premier league for table tennis). The video was published on August 8, 2019

claim: TableTennisDaily's 'Forehand Tomahawk Serve | Topspin' (May 2020) frames the serve as 'the axe serve' and stresses brushing up the back of the ball after getting low, ideal for a short side-topspin variant.
quote: the tomahawk serve (also called the axe serve) is a great serve that is easy to load up with side-topspin ... published on May 10, 2020

claim: TableTennisDaily's 'The EASIEST Serve to Learn - Tomahawk Tutorial' (Sep 2024) is the most recent mainstream beginner-friendly tutorial, pitching tomahawk as the lowest-effort serve to add to a club player's arsenal.
quote: The Tomahawk Serve has been a popular serve for many years throughout many levels of play (tutorial from September 1, 2024)

claim: William Henzell's tomahawk serve demo on ttEDGE (Mar 2015) shows a 3-time Olympian's slow, low, hard-to-attack short tomahawk - useful as a pro reference video for the motion.
quote: William Henzell is a 3-time Olympian who has become known for his distinctive tomahawk serve. He performs the tomahawk serve without putting a lot of speed on the ball, which makes it difficult to attack ... posted on YouTube on March 18, 2015

claim: Samson Dubina's 'The Forehand Tomahawk Serve' (Feb 2014) explains the right-to-left tip-up motion and explicitly recommends serving from the forehand side into the receiver's wide forehand for maximum break-away angle.
quote: done with the forehand, tip up, with a right-to-left motion, creating a spin that breaks to the left ... when done from the forehand side into the receiver's wide forehand, the motion allows maximum angle that really breaks into the receiver's wide forehand

claim: Expert Table Tennis describes the canonical tomahawk technique - lifting the elbow, extending the arm and 'imagining throwing an actual tomahawk' while contacting the upper-right rear of the ball and finishing under it - and recommends placing it short to the opponent's forehand 95% of the time.
quote: Lift your elbow and extend your arm outward, striking the ball with your forehand rubber ... imagining throwing an actual tomahawk ... contacting the ball at the upper right-hand corner of the ball from the rear ... serves 95% of the time to the opponent's forehand

claim: Larry Hodges (TableTennisCoaching.com) confirms the strategic value of a short tomahawk to the receiver's forehand: it produces 'tremendous sidespin that breaks away from the receiver,' forcing last-second lunges and errors.
quote: tremendous sidespin that breaks away from the receiver, frequently causing a last-second lunge as the receiver reaches for the ball, leading to many mistakes
